<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Big Data. Explain any four characteristics of Big Data with industrial examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Big Data is a term describing data sets of such massive volume, high velocity, and wide variety that traditional data management tools cannot process them efficiently within tolerable time limits.

The four key characteristics (Vs) are:

1. **Volume** — Refers to the massive size of data. A single Boeing 787 generates **~40 TB** of data per flight. Smart factories generate petabytes annually from sensors.
2. **Velocity** — The speed at which data is generated and must be processed. For example, autonomous vehicle sensors produce data at **>1 GB/second** requiring real-time analytics.
3. **Variety** — Different data formats coexist: structured (SQL tables), semi-structured (JSON logs), unstructured (camera images of defects).
4. **Value** — The ultimate goal is to extract business/mission-critical insight. **Veracity** (data trustworthiness) is often added as a fifth V to address noisy industrial data.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Descriptive and Predictive Analytics with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Descriptive Analytics | Predictive Analytics |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Purpose** | Answers *"What happened?"* | Answers *"What will happen?"* |
| **Technique** | Aggregation, dashboards, KPI reports | Regression, classification, time-series forecasting |
| **Output** | Historical reports | Future probability/score |
| **Example** | "Last quarter, Line 2 produced 50,000 units with 3% defects." | "Probability that Line 2 will exceed defect threshold in the next 7 days = 78%." |
| **Maturity Level** | Foundational (lower in DDPP pyramid) | Advanced (higher in DDPP pyramid) |

---

---

### 📝 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Big Data Analytics Reference Architecture** in an Industry 4.0 smart factory. Discuss the role of HDFS, MapReduce, and Apache Spark in this architecture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing IoT system generates **2.5 TB of raw sensor data per day**. The data is stored in HDFS with **block size = 256 MB** and **replication factor = 3**. Calculate:
1. The number of HDFS blocks created per day.
2. The total physical storage consumed per day.
3. The number of days of data that can be retained on a **15 TB storage cluster**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The Big Data Analytics Reference Architecture for an Industry 4.0 smart factory consists of **five integrated layers**:

**Layer 1 — Data Sources (Acquisition Layer):**
Includes IoT sensors on machines, PLCs, RFID tags, vision cameras, ERP/MES systems, and external feeds (supplier portals, weather APIs).

**Layer 2 — Data Ingestion Layer:**
Tools such as **Apache Kafka** (for high-throughput message streaming), **Apache Flume** (for log collection), and **MQTT brokers** handle the data ingress. They provide buffering, decouple producers from consumers, and guarantee delivery.

**Layer 3 — Distributed Storage Layer:**
**HDFS** stores the data across commodity nodes. Files are split into **256 MB blocks** (configurable; default 128 MB), each replicated **3 times** (default replication factor) for fault tolerance. The **NameNode** holds metadata; **DataNodes** hold actual blocks. **NoSQL databases** like HBase and Cassandra handle semi-structured sensor time-series.

**Layer 4 — Processing Layer:**
- **MapReduce** handles large-scale batch processing via two phases: **Map** (parallel processing → key-value pairs) and **Reduce** (aggregation per key). It is fault-tolerant and ideal for ETL.
- **Apache Spark** provides **in-memory cluster computing**, ideal for iterative Machine Learning algorithms. Spark's **MLlib** library contains scalable ML algorithms. Spark Streaming handles real-time micro-batches.

**Layer 5 — Analytics & Visualization Layer:**
This is where **Advanced Analytics** (DDPP) lives. Outputs feed **dashboards (Power BI, Grafana)**, **alerts (predictive maintenance tickets)**, and **closed-loop control signals** to the cyber-physical system.

> The key strength is the **closed-loop feedback** — analytics output can directly control physical actuators, achieving true Industry 4.0 autonomy.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Daily data = $D = 2.5$ TB
- Block size $B = 256$ MB
- Replication factor $R = 3$
- Cluster capacity $C = 15$ TB

**Step 1 — Convert data to MB.**

$$
D = 2.5\ \text{TB} = 2.5 \times 1024 \times 1024\ \text{MB} = 2{,}621{,}440\ \text{MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of blocks.**

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{2{,}621{,}440}{256} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 10{,}240 \right\rceil = 10{,}240\ \text{blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Total physical storage (with replication).**

$$
S_{total} = D \times R = 2.5\ \text{TB} \times 3 = 7.5\ \text{TB per day}
$$

**Step 4 — Retention days on 15 TB cluster.**

$$
N_{days} = \frac{C}{S_{daily}} = \frac{15}{7.5} = 2\ \text{days}
$$

The cluster can retain exactly **2 days** of data. After that, older blocks must be deleted (FIFO policy) or moved to **cold storage** (e.g., Amazon Glacier).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **four levels of Advanced Analytics (DDPP framework)** with one industrial example each. Discuss how each level adds value in a smart manufacturing environment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A predictive maintenance model for a CNC machine is evaluated on a test set of 800 machines. The results are:

|  | Predicted Failure | Predicted Healthy |
|--|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Actual Failure** | TP = 120 | FN = 30 |
| **Actual Healthy** | FP = 50 | TN = 600 |

Calculate the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score**, and interpret the model's performance. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The **DDPP Framework** (Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive) represents the maturity ladder of analytics in Industry 4.0.

**1. Descriptive Analytics — "What happened?"**
Aggregates historical data to produce KPIs and reports.
*Industrial Example:* "In Q3 2024, CNC Machine M-12 had an average spindle vibration of 4.2 mm/s and produced 9,500 parts with 2.8% scrap rate."
*Value:* Provides situational awareness to managers.

**2. Diagnostic Analytics — "Why did it happen?"**
Performs root-cause analysis using correlation, drill-down, and statistical tests.
*Industrial Example:* "Scrap spiked on Oct 12 because the ambient humidity exceeded 75% RH, causing thermal expansion in Tool T-7."
*Value:* Identifies corrective action targets.

**3. Predictive Analytics — "What will happen?"**
Uses ML models (regression, classification, time-series) to forecast future events.
*Industrial Example:* "Based on vibration trend, Spindle S-5 has an 88% probability of failure within 14 days."
*Value:* Enables proactive maintenance, reducing unplanned downtime by up to **50%**.

**4. Prescriptive Analytics — "What should we do?"**
Combines prediction with optimization (linear programming, RL) to recommend actions.
*Industrial Example:* "Replace S-5 spindle at the next scheduled downtime (Friday 18:00) to avoid an estimated $35,000 loss from emergency shutdown and scrap."
*Value:* Closes the cyber-physical loop, directly controlling industrial processes for maximum ROI.

> **Cumulative Value:** Each level *enables* the next. Prescriptive analytics is impossible without Predictive; Predictive requires Diagnostic data; Diagnostic requires Descriptive foundations.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given confusion matrix:**

|  | Predicted Failure | Predicted Healthy |
|--|-------------------|-------------------|
| Actual Failure | TP = 120 | FN = 30 |
| Actual Healthy | FP = 50 | TN = 600 |

**Step 1 — Accuracy.**

$$
Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{120 + 600}{800} = \frac{720}{800} = 0.90\ (90\%)
$$

**Step 2 — Precision.**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{120}{120 + 50} = \frac{120}{170} = 0.7059\ (70.59\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Recall.**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{120}{120 + 30} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80\ (80\%)
$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score.**

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7059 \times 0.80}{0.7059 + 0.80} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5647}{1.5059} = 2 \times 0.3750 = 0.75
$$

**Interpretation:**

- The model correctly classifies **90%** of all machines — good overall.
- However, **30 actual failures were missed (FN)** — in safety-critical Industry 4.0 applications, this is concerning.
- **Recall (80%) > Precision (70.59%)** — the model errs on the side of caution (catches most failures but raises some false alarms).
- **F1-Score of 0.75** indicates balanced but not excellent performance. The model should be retrained with additional sensor features (e.g., acoustic emission) to improve recall above **0.90** before deployment in production.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting replication factor** in HDFS storage calculations — examiners *deliberately* give such problems; failing to multiply by R loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Precision and Recall** — Precision = "of all alarms raised, how many were real?", Recall = "of all real failures, how many did we catch?". This is a **guaranteed trick question**.
> 3. **Using 1000 (decimal) instead of 1024 (binary)** in TB↔MB conversions — KTU expects **binary** by default (1 TB = 1024 GB = 1024² MB).
> 4. **Confusing MapReduce with Spark** — MapReduce writes intermediate results to **disk**; Spark keeps them **in memory** (hence ~100× faster for iterative algorithms).
> 5. **Mixing up Hadoop components** — NameNode = master metadata; DataNode = slave storage. YARN = resource manager (not data storage).
> 6. **Skipping the "5Vs" in Big Data definitions** — at least *three* Vs are required for full marks.
> 7. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — always write MB, TB, %, etc.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Big Data & Advanced Analysis**

✅ **Big Data** = data too large/vast/fast for traditional tools (5Vs: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value).

✅ **Types of Big Data:** Structured (SQL), Semi-structured (JSON/XML), Unstructured (images, video, text).

✅ **Hadoop Ecosystem** = HDFS (storage) + YARN (resource mgmt) + MapReduce (batch processing) + Spark (in-memory fast).

✅ **HDFS defaults:** Block size = **128 MB**; Replication factor = **3**; NameNode = master; DataNode = slave.

✅ **MapReduce phases:** Input Split → **Map** (key-value emit) → **Shuffle & Sort** (group by key) → **Reduce** (aggregate) → Output.

✅ **Apache Spark** = in-memory, **~100× faster** than MapReduce for ML; includes MLlib, Spark SQL, Spark Streaming.

✅ **NoSQL** types: Key-Value (Redis), Document (MongoDB), Column-Family (Cassandra/HBase), Graph (Neo4j).

✅ **DDPP Analytics Maturity:**
- Descriptive → "What happened?"
- Diagnostic → "Why?"
- Predictive → "What will happen?"
- Prescriptive → "What to do?"

✅ **Predictive model metrics:** Accuracy, Precision = TP/(TP+FP), Recall = TP/(TP+FN), F1 = 2PR/(P+R).

✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1-p) + p/n]$; max speedup = $1/(1-p)$ — sequential code is the bottleneck.

✅ **Storage formula:** $S_{total} = D \times R$; **Block count:** $N = \lceil D / B \rceil$.

✅ **Conversion factors:** 1 TB = 1024 GB = 1024² MB = 1024³ KB (binary, KTU default).

✅ **Industry 4.0 use cases:** Predictive maintenance, quality control via computer vision, digital twin synchronization, energy management, supply chain analytics.

✅ **Closed-loop CPS:** Analytics output → control signal → physical actuator → sensor feedback (the core of Industry 4.0 autonomy).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internet of Things (IoT), Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), and Internet of Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT832 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRY 4.0
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internet of Things (IoT), Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT), and Internet of Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Internet of Things (IoT)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Aligned to Industry 4.0 Framework):**
> The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a cyber-physical ecosystem of interconnected physical devices, vehicles, appliances, and other objects embedded with **sensors, software, actuators, and network connectivity** that enables these entities to **collect, exchange, and act upon data** over the Internet without requiring direct human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Nervous System"

Think of the human body. Your nervous system constantly gathers signals from your skin (sensors), sends them to the brain (cloud/processor), and triggers your hand to pull away from a hot surface (actuator). You don't consciously "operate" every reflex. **IoT works exactly like this for the physical world.** A *temperature sensor* in a cold storage warehouse detects a rise in temperature, sends the data over Wi-Fi/4G to a cloud server, which then automatically commands a *compressor (actuator)* to switch on. No human typed a single command — the "reflex" was automatic.

### Core Characteristics of IoT (KTU High-Yield Points)

- **Connectivity** — Devices connect to the Internet (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, NB-IoT).
- **Intelligence & Identity** — Each "thing" has a unique **IP/MAC address** and embedded intelligence (microcontrollers, edge AI).
- **Dynamic & Self-Adapting** — Devices adapt to changing context (e.g., smart thermostat learning user schedule).
- **Heterogeneity** — Different hardware, OS, and protocols coexist seamlessly.
- **Sensing Capability** — Real-world data capture via sensors (temperature, pressure, motion, gas).
- **Safety & Security** — Data privacy, authentication, and end-to-end encryption are baked in.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **three pillars** of any IoT system, as per KTU's reference framework, are: **Things (Sensors/Actuators)**, **Connectivity (Network)**, and **Intelligence (Cloud/Edge Computing)**. Every KTU question is mapped to one of these pillars.

### 1.1.1 IoT Reference Architecture (5-Layer Model)

| Layer | Function | Real-world Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception / Sensor Layer** | Captures physical parameters | DHT22 sensor in a smart fridge |
| **Network / Transport Layer** | Transmits data via protocols | Wi-Fi router, 4G/5G base station |
| **Middleware / Processing Layer** | Stores, filters, analyses data | AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub |
| **Application Layer** | Delivers user-facing service | Mobile app showing room temperature |
| **Business Layer** | Manages overall system, ROI, decisions | Dashboard for facility manager |

---

## 1.2 Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)** is a specialised subset of IoT applied to **industrial domains** such as manufacturing, energy, logistics, oil & gas, and utilities. It integrates **cyber-physical systems (CPS), cloud computing, cognitive computing, and big-data analytics** to create "smart factories" and "smart enterprises" that are self-optimising, self-configuring, and self-healing.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Factory"

A regular factory is like a car that needs a driver to steer, brake, and accelerate. An **IIoT-enabled factory** is like a **Tesla on Autopilot** — the car's cameras, radar, and GPS (sensors + connectivity) feed data to an AI brain (analytics + cloud), which decides when to brake at a red light (actuator) without human input. The factory predicts machine failures, reorders spare parts, and adjusts production speed — all autonomously.

### IIoT vs IoT — Critical Distinction for KTU Exams

| Parameter | **IoT** | **IIoT** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary User** | Consumers (B2C) | Industries / Enterprises (B2B) |
| **Goal** | Convenience & lifestyle | Efficiency, uptime, safety, ROI |
| **Criticality** | Tolerable failure | Zero-downtime / mission-critical |
| **Data Volume** | Small to moderate | Massive (Big Data) |
| **Reliability / Latency** | Best-effort | Real-time, deterministic (<10 ms) |
| **Security Standard** | Standard | Industrial-grade (IEC 62443, ISO 27001) |
| **Connectivity** | Wi-Fi, BLE, 4G | Industrial protocols: **OPC-UA, Modbus, Profinet, MQTT, TSN** |
| **Examples** | Smart watch, Alexa, Fitbit | Smart turbines, predictive maintenance, SCADA, digital twins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** "**IIoT = IoT + Reliability + Real-Time + Big Data + Safety**"

### IIoT Architecture — The Three-Tier Industrial Stack

1. **Edge / Device Tier** — PLCs, RTUs, smart sensors, robotic arms, vibration monitors.
2. **Platform / Network Tier** — Industrial gateways, OPC-UA servers, time-series databases, MQTT brokers.
3. **Cloud / Enterprise Tier** — Big-data lakes, ML models, ERP/MES integration, executive dashboards.

### Real-World Use Cases (Frequently Asked in KTU)

- **Predictive Maintenance** — Vibration sensors on a CNC machine predict bearing failure 30 days in advance.
- **Digital Twin** — A virtual replica of a physical turbine simulates "what-if" scenarios.
- **Energy Management** — Smart meters on a plant floor identify 18% energy waste during idle hours.
- **Supply Chain Visibility** — RFID + GPS track raw materials from supplier to assembly line.

---

## 1.3 Internet of Services (IoS)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (From Module 2, Industry 4.0 Framework):**
> The **Internet of Services (IoS)** is a paradigm where **services (rather than physical products) are delivered, combined, and consumed over the Internet**. It transforms traditional products into a bundle of value-added services, enabling business models based on **pay-per-use, subscription, and outcome-based pricing**. It is a cornerstone of Industry 4.0, alongside IoT, IIoT, and Cyber-Physical Systems.

### Conceptual Analogy — From "Owning a Car" to "Subscribing to Mobility"

In the 20th century, you **bought a car** (product). In the 21st century, you tap your phone and an Uber arrives — you paid for **mobility as a service**, not the asset itself. The car's engine, GPS, IoT sensors, and cloud routing all worked together. **IoS is the same idea applied to everything: products become platforms, customers become subscribers, and revenue shifts from one-time sale to recurring streams.**

### Core Service Categories in IoS

- **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** — e.g., AWS EC2 providing virtual servers.
- **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** — e.g., Microsoft Azure IoT Hub providing a development environment.
- **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** — e.g., Salesforce, Google Workspace.
- **Everything-as-a-Service (XaaS)** — e.g., Rolls-Royce "Power-by-the-Hour" — airlines pay for engine thrust, not the engine.
- **Manufacturing-as-a-Service (MaaS)** — On-demand 3D printing of parts.

### IoS Characteristics (Exam-Oriented)

1. **Service-Orientation** — Modular, reusable, interoperable service components.
2. **Cloud-Based Delivery** — Services hosted on distributed cloud infrastructure.
3. **Customisation & Personalisation** — Services dynamically configured per user.
4. **Pay-per-Use Billing** — Metered consumption, subscription, or outcome pricing.
5. **Cross-Organisational Integration** — Services from multiple providers combine into composite offerings.
6. **Standardised Protocols** — REST APIs, SOAP, gRPC, web services, OpenAPI.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** The Industry 4.0 Reference Model (RAMI 4.0) places **IoS as the top horizontal layer** that consumes data from **IIoT** running over **IoT infrastructure**. The "things" sense, the "industrial layer" processes, the "services" monetise.

### Real-World Examples of IoS in Industry 4.0

| Industry | Traditional (Product) | IoS Model (Service) |
|---|---|---|
| **Aviation** | Sell aircraft engine | Rolls-Royce: "Power-by-the-Hour" |
| **Automotive** | Sell car | Volvo: "Care by Volvo" subscription |
| **Printing** | Sell printer | HP: "Instant Ink" — pay per page |
| **Lighting** | Sell bulbs | Philips: "Pay-per-Lux" lighting as a service |
| **Manufacturing** | Sell CNC machine | Trumpf: "Pay-per-Part" sheet-metal cutting |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The IoT Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

To truly master KTU exam questions, you must memorise **the exact 5-stage data flow** within an IoT system. Examiners frequently test this as a "draw and explain" or "describe the working" question worth 7–10 marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 1 → 5 Rule:** "**SENSE → TRANSMIT → STORE → ANALYSE → ACT**" (Mnemonic: *S-T-S-A-A*)

### Step 1: Sensing (Perception Layer)
- Sensors (temperature, humidity, gas, motion, IR, pressure) convert physical phenomena into **analogue electrical signals**.
- **Transducers** convert signals into a digital format readable by microcontrollers (e.g., ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi).
- **Key Metric Sampled:** Sampling rate, resolution (in bits), and accuracy (±°C).

### Step 2: Aggregation at the Edge
- The microcontroller packages data into a structured format (typically **JSON or CBOR**).
- **Edge computing** may pre-process data to reduce bandwidth (e.g., only transmit if temperature > 30°C).

### Step 3: Transmission (Network Layer)
- Data is sent over a communication protocol. The choice of protocol depends on:
  - **Range** (Bluetooth ~10 m, Wi-Fi ~50 m, LoRa ~10 km, 5G ~cell-wide).
  - **Power budget** (battery-powered sensors prefer BLE or LoRa).
  - **Latency requirement** (real-time control needs 5G URLLC or TSN).

### Step 4: Processing & Storage (Middleware Layer)
- Cloud platforms (**AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT**) receive the data stream.
- Stored in **time-series databases** (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) for analytics.

### Step 5: Action & Feedback (Application + Actuation Layer)
- Analytics engines (ML models, threshold alerts) generate insights.
- A command is sent back to the **actuator** (relay, motor, valve, robotic arm) to perform a physical action.
- The user receives notification via dashboard / app.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> A smart greenhouse has **100 soil-moisture sensors** sampling at **1 Hz** (1 sample per second), each producing a **16-bit (2-byte)** packet. Calculate the daily data volume generated and the required minimum bandwidth.

**Solution Outline (covered in full in Section 3).**

---

## 2.2 IoT Communication Protocols — The Protocol Stack

KTU frequently asks: *"Compare MQTT and HTTP for IoT."* Memorise this comparison table.

| Parameter | **HTTP / REST** | **MQTT** | **CoAP** | **AMQP** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Client-Server | Publish-Subscribe (Broker) | Client-Server (UDP) | Pub-Sub (Broker) |
| **Transport** | TCP | TCP | UDP | TCP |
| **Header Size** | Large (~hundreds of bytes) | Tiny (2-byte fixed header) | Tiny (4-byte header) | Medium (8-byte frame) |
| **Power Consumption** | High | Low | Very Low | Medium |
| **Best For** | Web dashboards, low-frequency apps | Battery sensors, mobile push | Constrained devices, multicasting | Enterprise messaging, banking |
| **QoS Levels** | None | 0, 1, 2 | Confirmable / Non-confirmable | 0, 1, 2 |
| **Example Use** | Smart-home app backend | Industrial telemetry | Smart agriculture sensors | Financial transaction messaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** "Why is MQTT preferred over HTTP for IoT?"
> **Model Answer:** HTTP uses verbose text-based headers, runs over TCP with a heavy handshake, and follows a request-response model — inefficient for low-bandwidth, high-latency, battery-powered sensors. MQTT uses a compact binary 2-byte header, runs over a persistent TCP connection, and uses a publish-subscribe broker model where the device only sends data when needed. This makes MQTT **~10× more bandwidth-efficient and energy-efficient**, ideal for IIoT telemetry.

---

## 2.3 IIoT Reference Architecture — The "Three-Tier + Two-Cross-Cutting" Model

The Industrial Internet Consortium (IIC) defines the IIoT Reference Architecture as **three tiers** with **two cross-cutting concerns** woven through them.

| Layer / Concern | Function | KTU-Relevant Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge Tier** | Sensors, actuators, controllers, PLCs | Data acquisition, pre-processing, local control loops |
| **Platform Tier** | Gateways, MQTT brokers, OPC-UA servers, time-series DBs | Protocol translation, data normalisation, buffering |
| **Enterprise Tier** | Cloud analytics, ML, ERP/MES, dashboards | Big-data analytics, ML model hosting, business decisions |
| **⚙️ Cross-Cutting: Connectivity** | Wired (Ethernet/IP, Profinet) + Wireless (5G, Wi-Fi 6) | Seamless IT/OT convergence |
| **🔒 Cross-Cutting: Security & Privacy** | Identity, authentication, encryption, audit | Zero-trust, IEC 62443, defence-in-depth |

### Why IIoT Demands a "Different" Architecture

- **Real-time Determinism** — A robotic arm on a production line cannot tolerate 500 ms of jitter. IIoT uses **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** and **OPC-UA over TSN** to guarantee bounded latency.
- **Legacy Interoperability** — Factories have 30-year-old PLCs. IIoT platforms must speak **Modbus, Profibus, BACnet** in addition to modern protocols.
- **Safety Integrity** — Standards like **IEC 61508 (SIL)** and **ISO 13849 (PL)** govern safety-critical IIoT deployments.

---

## 2.4 IoS — The Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) Foundations

The Internet of Services is built on **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** principles. To score full marks in KTU, you must define SOA and explain the four core properties.

### The Four Pillars of SOA

1. **Loose Coupling** — Services are independent; changes in one service don't break others.
2. **Reusability** — A service built once can be consumed by multiple applications.
3. **Abstraction** — Consumers don't need to know the internal implementation of a service.
4. **Composability** — Complex services are built by combining simpler ones (service orchestration).

### Web Services Enablers of IoS

| Technology | Role in IoS | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|
| **WSDL** | Describes what a service does (interface contract) | XML-based, formal definition |
| **SOAP** | Protocol for exchanging service messages | Heavy, enterprise-grade |
| **REST (Representational State Transfer)** | Lightweight, HTTP-based, JSON-friendly | Most popular for modern IoS |
| **UDDI** | Universal directory to discover services | Yellow pages of services |
| **API Gateway** | Manages, secures, and routes service calls | Modern replacement for UDDI |

### IoS Business Model Transformation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Product-Service Continuum:**
> In Industry 4.0, every product becomes a *service-enabler*. KTU exams love the phrase **"Servitisation"** — the strategic shift from selling products to selling integrated product-service bundles. **Servitisation + IoT data = XaaS (Everything-as-a-Service).**

### The IoS Value Chain (4 Layers)

1. **Infrastructure Providers** — Cloud vendors (AWS, Azure, GCP).
2. **Platform Providers** — Integration, analytics, AI services.
3. **Service Providers** — Industry-specific solutions (e.g., Siemens MindSphere for manufacturing).
4. **Service Consumers** — End customers consuming the bundled offering.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Data & Performance Metrics)

Although IoT/IIoT/IoS is conceptual, KTU often asks simple numerical questions on **data volume, bandwidth, and latency**. The key formulas are:

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Daily Data Volume** | $V = N \times S \times R \times 86400$ | Bytes/day | $N$ = number of sensors, $S$ = sample size (bytes), $R$ = sampling rate (Hz) |
| **Bandwidth Required** | $BW = \dfrac{V_{\text{per second}}}{10^6}$ | Mbps | Divide by $10^6$ for megabits/sec |
| **Latency Budget** | $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{net}} + L_{\text{process}} + L_{\text{act}}$ | ms (milliseconds) | Real-time control: $L_{\text{total}} < 10$ ms |
| **Reliability / Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$ | % | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures; MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| **Cost per Bit** | $C_b = \dfrac{C_{\text{device}}}{L \times V_{\text{total}}}$ | ₹/bit | $L$ = device lifetime (years), $V_{\text{total}}$ = total bytes transmitted |
| **Battery Life (Years)** | $B_{yr} = \dfrac{C_{\text{battery}} \times V_{\text{battery}}}{I_{\text{avg}} \times 86400 \times 365}$ | years | $I_{\text{avg}}$ = average current draw (A); used in LPWAN design |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Table Rule Reminder:** All absolute-value / division bars in formulas are rendered as **LaTeX fractions `\dfrac{}{}`** rather than vertical pipes, to keep the table syntactically valid.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Daily Data Volume & Bandwidth for a Sensor Network

This is the most frequently asked numerical problem in KTU Module 2. We will solve it **exhaustively**, with every algebraic step justified.

### Problem Statement
> A smart agriculture deployment uses **120 soil-moisture sensors**, each sampling at **0.5 Hz** (one sample every 2 seconds), with each sample being a **32-bit (4-byte) float** value. The data is transmitted every minute as a batched JSON packet with a **fixed header of 200 bytes**.
> **(a)** Calculate the total raw data volume generated per day.
> **(b)** Calculate the average bandwidth required in **kbps** during a 1-hour active transmission window.
> **(c)** If the network uses **LoRaWAN (SF=7, 250 kHz channel, bitrate 5.5 kbps)**, determine how many such deployments can share the channel simultaneously without exceeding 80% capacity.

---

### Part (a) — Total Raw Data Volume per Day

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 120 \quad \text{(number of sensors)} \\
R_s &= 0.5 \;\text{Hz} \quad \text{(sampling rate per sensor)} \\
S_b &= 4 \;\text{bytes} \quad \text{(size of one float sample)} \\
T &= 86400 \;\text{s} \quad \text{(seconds in a day)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute samples per second from the entire sensor array.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{total}} &= N \times R_s \\
&= 120 \times 0.5 \\
&= 60 \;\text{samples/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Since each of the 120 sensors contributes 0.5 samples per second, the aggregate sampling rate is simply the product of sensor count and individual sampling rate.

**Step 3: Compute raw data volume per second (excluding headers).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{raw per second}} &= R_{\text{total}} \times S_b \\
&= 60 \times 4 \\
&= 240 \;\text{bytes/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to per-day raw volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{raw per day}} &= V_{\text{raw per second}} \times T \\
&= 240 \times 86400 \\
&= 20{,}736{,}000 \;\text{bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to MB/day for readability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{MB}} &= \dfrac{20{,}736{,}000}{10^6} \\
&= 20.736 \;\text{MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Part (b) — Average Bandwidth in kbps

**Step 1: Add the JSON header overhead to the per-second volume.**

Assuming each sensor sends a packet of 1 sample (4 B) every 2 seconds, the header is amortised. For simplicity, treat the header as a fixed daily overhead:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{header per day}} &= 200 \;\text{bytes} \times 60 \;\text{minutes} \times 24 \;\text{hours} \\
&= 200 \times 1440 \\
&= 288{,}000 \;\text{bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total daily data volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= V_{\text{raw}} + V_{\text{header}} \\
&= 20{,}736{,}000 + 288{,}000 \\
&= 21{,}024{,}000 \;\text{bytes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total per-second volume (averaged over 24 hours).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{per second}} &= \dfrac{21{,}024{,}000}{86400} \\
&= 243.33 \;\text{bytes/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to bits per second, then to kbps.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{bits/s}} &= 243.33 \times 8 \\
&= 1946.67 \;\text{bits/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{\text{kbps}} &= \dfrac{1946.67}{1000} \\
&\approx 1.947 \;\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: If the data is only transmitted in a 1-hour active window (e.g., uplink burst every hour), the *peak* bandwidth is:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{\text{peak, kbps}} &= \dfrac{1.947 \times 24}{1} \\
&= 46.73 \;\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The same daily data is squeezed into 1 hour, so we multiply by 24 (the compression factor in time). This is the peak bandwidth the gateway must support.

---

### Part (c) — Channel Capacity & Concurrent Deployments on LoRaWAN

**Step 1: Maximum safe channel occupancy (80% of 5.5 kbps).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{\text{safe}} &= 0.80 \times 5500 \\
&= 4400 \;\text{bps} = 4.4 \;\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Number of deployments that can share the channel.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{deployments}} &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{BW_{\text{safe}}}{BW_{\text{peak, kbps}} \times 1000} \right\rfloor \\
&= \left\lfloor \dfrac{4400}{46.73} \right\rfloor \\
&= \left\lfloor 94.16 \right\rfloor \\
&= 94 \;\text{deployments}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Interpretation:* Up to **94 such smart-agriculture deployments** can simultaneously share a single LoRaWAN channel (SF=7) while keeping the channel usage below the recommended 80% threshold — a critical consideration for IoT network planners.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: Latency Budget for an IIoT Closed-Loop Control

> **Problem:** A robotic arm on an assembly line uses an IIoT vision system to detect misaligned parts. Calculate the **end-to-end latency budget** to ensure the arm can correct within **one production cycle of 1.2 seconds**, given the following sub-system latencies:
> - Sensor (camera) capture: $L_{\text{sense}} = 15$ ms
> - Image pre-processing at edge GPU: $L_{\text{process}} = 80$ ms
> - 5G URLLC uplink + downlink round-trip: $L_{\text{net}} = 4$ ms
> - Actuator (servo motor) command execution: $L_{\text{act}} = 20$ ms
> - Safety watchdog overhead: $L_{\text{watchdog}} = 5$ ms

**Step 1: Identify the total latency budget equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{process}} + L_{\text{net}} + L_{\text{act}} + L_{\text{watchdog}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} = 15 + 80 + 4 + 20 + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the terms.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} = 124 \;\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compare against the production cycle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle time available} &= 1200 \;\text{ms} \\
\text{Latency used} &= 124 \;\text{ms} \\
\text{Slack / Safety margin} &= 1200 - 124 = 1076 \;\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute utilisation ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \dfrac{L_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{124}{1200} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 10.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Engineering Interpretation:* The IIoT control loop uses only **~10% of the available cycle time**, leaving 90% margin for network jitter, retransmissions, and edge-AI model inference variability. This is a **healthy, real-time-safe** design — below the typical 30% utilisation threshold recommended for industrial control.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use IoT vs IIoT vs IoS

| Scenario | Best Fit | Why |
|---|---|---|
| A consumer wants a fitness band | **IoT** | Low cost, consumer-grade, convenience-driven, intermittent failure is OK |
| A power plant needs real-time turbine monitoring | **IIoT** | Mission-critical, real-time, high reliability, industrial protocols required |
| A factory wants to predict motor failure | **IIoT + IoS** | Sensors + analytics (IIoT) deliver an "Uptime-as-a-Service" subscription (IoS) |
| A logistics company tracks shipments via GPS | **IoT + IoS** | Low-cost GPS trackers (IoT) feed a "Logistics-Visibility-as-a-Service" dashboard (IoS) |
| A hospital tracks infusion pump status | **IIoT** | Patient safety demands deterministic, secure, regulated connectivity |
| A startup sells "Cold-Chain-as-a-Service" to pharma | **IoT + IIoT + IoS** | Sensors (IoT) + industrial-grade reliability (IIoT) + subscription model (IoS) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** "If the question mentions *sensor, consumer, smartphone* → **IoT**. If it mentions *factory, PLC, latency, predictive maintenance* → **IIoT**. If it mentions *subscription, pay-per-use, AWS, cloud* → **IoS**. If it mentions *Industry 4.0* holistically → answer covers all three."
