| 1 | $L_{total} = L_{prop} + L_{trans} + L_{proc} + L_{queue}$ | Total end-to-end latency (propagation + transmission + processing + queuing) | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $L_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_{signal}}$ | Propagation latency = distance ÷ signal velocity | s |
| 3 | $L_{trans} = \dfrac{P_{size}}{R_{link}}$ | Transmission latency = packet size ÷ link rate | s |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{P_{size} \cdot N_{pkts}}{\Delta t}$ | Throughput = total data delivered in interval | bps |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability = Mean Time Between Failures / (MTBF + Mean Time To Repair) | ratio (0–1) |
| 6 | $P_{loss}(\%) = \dfrac{N_{lost}}{N_{sent}} \times 100$ | Packet loss percentage | % |
| 7 | $J = \sigma(L_i)$ | Jitter = standard deviation of latency samples | s |
| 8 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (wireless CPS) | dB |
| 9 | $BER \approx \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{SNR}\right)$ | Bit Error Rate approximation (BPSK in AWGN) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon-Hartley channel capacity | bps |
| 11 | $R_{effective} = \dfrac{P_{size} - H_{overhead}}{T_{cycle}}$ | Effective payload rate in deterministic cycle | bps |
| 12 | $\text{Determinism Ratio} = \dfrac{L_{max, observed}}{L_{bound, guaranteed}}$ | Must be ≤ 1 for hard real-time CPS | dimensionless |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

CPS networking underpins:
- **Smart factories** (Siemens Amberg, Bosch Rexroth)
- **Autonomous vehicles** (V2X communication)
- **Smart grids** (IEC 61850 substation networks)
- **Medical CPS** (closed-loop insulin pumps, robotic surgery)
- **Smart cities** (traffic CPS, water distribution CPS)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total End-to-End Latency in a CPS Network

**Goal:** Express the total latency a CPS packet experiences traversing $N$ network nodes (sensors → edge → cloud → controller).

**Step 1 — Define the latency components at every node:**

At each intermediate node $i$, the packet experiences four sequential delays:

$$
L_{node,i} = L_{prop,i} + L_{trans,i} + L_{proc,i} + L_{queue,i}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all N nodes in the path:**

$$
L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl(L_{prop,i} + L_{trans,i} + L_{proc,i} + L_{queue,i}\bigr)
$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate by delay type:**

$$
L_{total} = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{prop,i}}_{L_{prop}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{trans,i}}_{L_{trans}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{proc,i}}_{L_{proc}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{queue,i}}_{L_{queue}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express propagation as distance over signal velocity:**

For node $i$ with cable length $d_i$ and signal propagation speed $v$ (≈ 2 × 10⁸ m/s in fiber):

$$
L_{prop,i} = \frac{d_i}{v}
$$

Hence:

$$
L_{prop} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{d_i}{v} = \frac{D}{v}, \quad \text{where } D = \sum d_i
$$

**Step 5 — Express transmission as packet size over link rate:**

For node $i$ with packet size $P_i$ bits and link rate $R_i$ bps:

$$
L_{trans,i} = \frac{P_i}{R_i}
$$

Hence:

$$
L_{trans} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{P_i}{R_i}
$$

**Step 6 — Express queuing delay using M/M/1 queue approximation:**

For a node with utilization $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ (arrival rate / service rate), under Poisson traffic:

$$
L_{queue} = \frac{\rho}{\mu (1 - \rho)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}
$$

**Step 7 — Final consolidated formula:**

$$
\boxed{L_{total} \;=\; \frac{D}{v} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{P_i}{R_i} \;+\; N \cdot L_{proc} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\mu_i - \lambda_i}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** For hard real-time CPS, the **maximum observed** $L_{total}$ must be ≤ the **deadline** $D_{deadline}$. If even one packet violates this, the CPS fails its safety certification (e.g., IEC 61508 SIL-3).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A CPS sensor samples 256 bytes of data and transmits over a 1 Gbps industrial Ethernet link to a PLC, then to an edge gateway, then to a cloud server. Cable spans: 50 m + 100 m + 5 km. Processing delay at each node = 20 µs. Arrival rate at the gateway λ = 800 packets/s, service rate µ = 1000 packets/s. Compute the total latency.

**Step 1 — Propagation:**

$$
L_{prop} = \frac{50 + 100 + 5000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{5150}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 25.75\ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission (same packet size, same 1 Gbps link):**

$$
L_{trans} = 3 \times \frac{256 \times 8}{10^{9}} = 3 \times 2.048\ \mu s = 6.144\ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Processing:**

$$
L_{proc} = 3 \times 20\ \mu s = 60\ \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Queuing at the gateway (M/M/1):**

$$
L_{queue} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{1000 - 800} = \frac{1}{200} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ s = 5000\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Total:**

$$
L_{total} = 25.75 + 6.144 + 60 + 5000 \approx 5091.89\ \mu s \approx 5.09\ ms
$$

**Conclusion:** The gateway is the **bottleneck** (queuing dominates). For a hard real-time CPS requiring ≤ 1 ms latency, this design is **unacceptable** — the gateway must be upgraded or traffic offloaded to TSN.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Selection for CPS

| CPS Application | Recommended Protocol | Justification | Target Latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Robotic arm control | EtherCAT | Sub-100 µs, processing-on-the-fly | **< 100 µs** |
| Automotive ECU network | CAN FD | Robust, low cost, 5 Mbps | **< 1 ms** |
| Plant-wide monitoring | OPC UA over TSN | Standards-compliant, semantic | **< 10 ms** |
| Smart meter telemetry | MQTT over LTE | Lightweight, cloud-friendly | **< 1 s** |
| Wearable health CPS | BLE | Low-power, short-range | **< 100 ms** |
| V2X autonomous driving | 5G URLLC / C-V2X | High mobility, low latency | **< 5 ms** |
| Substation automation | IEC 61850 / GOOSE | Hard real-time, peer-to-peer | **< 4 ms** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical CPS Network Architecture (Industry 4.0 Five-Layer Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L5["LAYER 5 ENTERPRISE AND CLOUD"]
        A1[ERP and AI Analytics]
        A2[Digital Twin Platform]
    end

    subgraph L4["LAYER 4 OPERATIONS AND PLANT"]
        B1[SCADA System]
        B2[MES Manufacturing Execution]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3 CELL AND STATION"]
        C1[Edge Gateway OPC UA]
        C2[MQTT Broker]
        C3[Time Sensitive Network Switch]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2 CONTROL"]
        D1[Programmable Logic Controller A]
        D2[Programmable Logic Controller B]
        D3[Motion Controller]
    end

    subgraph L1["LAYER 1 FIELD AND DEVICE"]
        E1[Sensors Temperature Pressure Vibration]
        E2[Actuators Motors Valves]
        E3[Radio Frequency Identification Tags]
    end

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    C3 <--> D1
    C3 <--> D2
    C3 <--> D3
    C1 <--> C2
```

## 4.2 CPS Communication Protocol Stack (Layered Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER OPC UA MQTT COAP"]
        F1[Semantic Data Exchange]
        F2[Industrial Dashboards]
    end

    subgraph TRAN["TRANSPORT LAYER TCP UDP TSN"]
        G1[Reliable Streams]
        G2[Time Critical Delivery]
    end

    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER IPv4 IPv6 5G"]
        H1[Routing and Addressing]
        H2[Ultra Reliable Low Latency]
    end

    subgraph LINK["LINK LAYER ETHERCAT PROFINET CAN WIRELESSHART"]
        I1[Deterministic Cycle]
        I2[Mesh Mesh Networking]
    end

    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL LAYER COPPER FIBER WIRELESS"]
        J1[Ethernet Cables]
        J2[Radio Frequency Channels]
    end

    APP --> TRAN
    TRAN --> NET
    NET --> LINK
    LINK --> PHYS
```

## 4.3 CPS Network Data Flow (Bidirectional Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSOR[Physical Sensor Temperature] -->|Analog to Digital| NODE1[Sensor Node CPS]
    NODE1 -->|MQTT over Wi Fi| EDGE[Edge Gateway]
    NODE1 -->|EtherCAT Cable| PLC[Programmable Logic Controller]
    PLC -->|Deterministic Command| ACT[Actuator Motor]
    EDGE -->|OPC UA over TSN| CLOUD[Cloud Server and Digital Twin]
    CLOUD -->|Analytics Feedback| EDGE
    EDGE -->|Control Update| PLC
    ACT -->|Physical Action| PROCESS[Industrial Process]
    PROCESS -->|Feedback| SENSOR
```

## 4.4 Deterministic vs. Non-Deterministic Network Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DET["DETERMINISTIC CPS NETWORK"]
        D1[Bounded Latency Guaranteed]
        D2[Time Synchronized PTP IEEE 1588]
        D3[Scheduled Traffic TSN]
    end

    subgraph NONDET["NON DETERMINISTIC BEST EFFORT"]
        N1[Variable Latency]
        N2[No Time Sync]
        N3[Contention Based]
    end

    DET --> SAFE[Suitable for Safety Critical CPS]
    NONDET --> UNSAFE[Unsuitable for Hard Real Time]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** Define Cyber-Physical System (CPS) networking. List any four key requirements of a CPS network.

**Model Answer:**
CPS networking is the communication infrastructure that interlinks physical processes (sensors, actuators, machines) with cyber elements (controllers, cloud, AI) to enable real-time monitoring and control in Industry 4.0.

**Four key requirements:**
1. **Determinism** – worst-case latency must be bounded.
2. **Real-time capability** – millisecond or sub-millisecond response.
3. **Reliability** – 99.999% availability for safety-critical applications.
4. **Security-by-design** – authentication, encryption, and integrity protection.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** Differentiate between MQTT and CoAP protocols in the context of CPS networking.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP |
| Pattern | Publish/Subscribe | Request/Response (RESTful) |
| Overhead | Moderate header | Very low header |
| Use Case | Edge-to-cloud reliable telemetry | Constrained node-to-node CPS |
| QoS Levels | 0, 1, 2 | Confirmable / Non-confirmable |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **five-layer hierarchical CPS network architecture** used in Industry 4.0. For each layer, identify one suitable communication protocol.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A CPS node transmits a 1 KB packet over a 100 Mbps link. The link spans 2 km, the processing delay at each of 3 nodes is 30 µs, and queuing follows M/M/1 with λ = 600 packets/s and µ = 1200 packets/s. Compute the total latency.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Layer 1 – Field/Device Layer:** Sensors, actuators, RFID tags → **IO-Link** (simple point-to-point).

**Layer 2 – Control Layer:** PLCs, motion controllers → **EtherCAT** (deterministic, sub-100 µs cycle).

**Layer 3 – Cell/Station Layer:** Machine-to-machine communication → **OPC UA** (semantic interoperability).

**Layer 4 – Operations Layer:** SCADA, MES → **PROFINET / Industrial Ethernet**.

**Layer 5 – Enterprise/Cloud Layer:** Analytics, AI, ERP → **MQTT over TLS** to cloud brokers.

Each layer's protocol is chosen to balance **latency, determinism, and interoperability**.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Propagation latency (2 km fiber):**

$$
L_{prop} = \frac{2000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 10\ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission latency (1 KB over 100 Mbps):**

$$
L_{trans} = 3 \times \frac{1024 \times 8}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 3 \times 81.92\ \mu s = 245.76\ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Processing latency:**

$$
L_{proc} = 3 \times 30\ \mu s = 90\ \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Queuing latency (M/M/1):**

$$
L_{queue} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{1200 - 600} = \frac{1}{600} = 1666.67\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Total latency:**

$$
L_{total} = 10 + 245.76 + 90 + 1666.67 \approx 2012.43\ \mu s \approx 2.01\ ms
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** and explain how it enables deterministic communication over standard Ethernet in CPS.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, illustrate the **bidirectional data flow** in a CPS involving sensors, edge gateway, cloud, and actuator. Identify the protocol used at each interface.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** is a set of IEEE 802.1 standards that add **determinism** to standard Ethernet, making it suitable for hard real-time CPS.

**Key TSN mechanisms:**
1. **IEEE 802.1Qbv – Time-Aware Shaper (TAS):** Schedules traffic into time slots using a global time base.
2. **IEEE 802.1Qbu – Frame Preemption:** Allows high-priority frames to interrupt low-priority frames.
3. **IEEE 802.1CB – Frame Replication and Elimination:** Provides seamless redundancy.
4. **IEEE 802.1AS – Time Synchronization:** Sub-microsecond clock sync using generalized Precision Time Protocol (gPTP).

**Why TSN matters for CPS:** Standard Ethernet is non-deterministic (CSMA/CD). TSN transforms it into a **deterministic, converged network** carrying both best-effort IT traffic and hard real-time OT traffic on the same wire — eliminating the need for separate industrial fieldbuses.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

Refer to **Section 4.3** of these notes (CPS Network Data Flow Block Diagram).

**Interface-level protocol identification:**

| Interface | Protocol |
|---|---|
| Sensor → Sensor Node | Analog / I²C / SPI |
| Sensor Node → Edge Gateway | MQTT over Wi-Fi |
| Sensor Node → PLC | EtherCAT |
| Edge Gateway → Cloud | OPC UA over TLS / MQTT |
| PLC → Actuator | EtherCAT / PROFINET IRT |
| Cloud → Edge | OPC UA Pub/Sub |
| Edge → PLC | OPC UA Client/Server |

This layered protocol selection ensures **determinism at the field level** and **interoperability at the enterprise level**.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing CPS with IoT** — IoT is *data collection*; CPS is *closed-loop control*. Examiners deduct marks if the difference is not articulated.
> 2. **Skipping units in latency calculations** — Always write µs or ms explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting queuing delay** — Students often calculate only propagation + transmission; examiners expect **all four** components.
> 4. **Using "faster = better" reasoning** — Determinism (bounded latency) matters more than raw speed.
> 5. **Not mentioning IEC 61508 / SIL** when discussing safety-critical CPS networks.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** in long answers — Block diagrams carry 2–3 marks even if the text is incomplete.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ CPS = **Cyber + Physical + Networking** — not just IoT.
- ✅ **Five-layer architecture**: Field → Control → Cell → Operations → Enterprise.
- ✅ **Determinism > Speed** — bounded latency is the gold standard.
- ✅ **Key wired protocols**: EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT, EtherNet/IP, CAN FD, OPC UA.
- ✅ **Key wireless protocols**: WirelessHART, 5G URLLC, BLE, LoRaWAN.
- ✅ **Edge-to-cloud protocols**: MQTT (TCP, pub/sub), CoAP (UDP, RESTful), AMQP.
- ✅ **Latency formula**: $L_{total} = L_{prop} + L_{trans} + L_{proc} + L_{queue}$.
- ✅ **Availability formula**: $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ → five-nines = 99.999%.
- ✅ **TSN** (IEEE 802.1) = deterministic Ethernet → enables converged IT/OT networks.
- ✅ **Shannon capacity**: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — theoretical max data rate.
- ✅ **Safety standards to mention**: IEC 61508, IEC 62443 (industrial cybersecurity), ISO 26262 (automotive CPS).
- ✅ **Typical cycle times**: EtherCAT ≈ 12.5 µs; PROFINET IRT ≈ 31.25 µs; CAN ≈ 1 ms; standard Ethernet ≈ best-effort (variable).
- ✅ **Digital Twin** integration requires **OPC UA over TSN** as the semantic backbone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wearable Cyber Physical Systems and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT832 - INTRODUCTION TO INDUSTRY 4.0
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wearable Cyber Physical Systems and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wearable Cyber-Physical System (WCPS):** A Wearable Cyber-Physical System is a closed-loop, body-area integrated computational platform that seamlessly fuses **sensing**, **computation**, **communication**, and **actuation** subsystems to monitor, analyse, and influence physical phenomena on or around the human body in real time. It is formally classified as a sub-domain of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** under the Industry 4.0 reference architecture, where the *cyber* (software/algorithmic) and *physical* (sensors, actuators, human body) worlds are bidirectionally coupled through continuous feedback loops.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OERUT832)**, a WCPS is positioned as a *human-centric CPS* where the human body itself forms a part of the physical loop. The cyber layer runs inference algorithms (often on edge microcontrollers or low-power AI accelerators), while the physical layer interacts with physiological, kinematic, and environmental variables.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Smart Bodyguard":**
> Imagine you hired an invisible, always-on personal bodyguard who lives *inside your shirt*. This bodyguard:
> - **Feels** your heartbeat, sweat, and movement (sensing).
> - **Thinks** about whether the readings are normal or dangerous (computation).
> - **Talks** to your phone, the cloud, and other nearby devices (communication).
> - **Acts** by vibrating on your wrist, flashing an alert, or even injecting insulin (actuation).
> 
> A Wearable CPS is exactly this bodyguard — a tiny, intelligent, networked computer worn on the body that turns raw biological/physical signals into smart, automated actions.

### 1.3 Core Components of a Wearable CPS

| Component | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensors** | Convert physical/physiological signals to electrical signals | PPG, ECG, EMG, IMU, temperature, galvanic skin response |
| **Processing Unit** | Runs inference, filtering, decision logic | ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, low-power AI SoCs |
| **Communication Module** | Wireless data exchange | BLE, Wi-Fi, NFC, LoRa, Zigbee, UWB |
| **Actuators** | Convert decisions back to physical action | Haptic motors, LEDs, displays, micro-pumps |
| **Power Source** | Energy supply | Li-ion, Li-Po, thin-film batteries, energy harvesters |

### 1.4 Key Design Metrics (Mandatory Bold Highlights)

- **Form Factor** — must be lightweight, flexible, and ergonomic.
- **Power Budget** — typically **< 50 mW** for continuous operation.
- **Latency** — medical-grade WCPS demand **< 100 ms** end-to-end response.
- **Sampling Rate** — physiological signals like ECG require **≥ 250 Hz** (per Nyquist criterion).
- **Data Security** — HIPAA/GDPR-compliant encryption is mandatory for biomedical data.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Wearable CPS is treated as the *bridge* between the **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**, **Edge AI**, and **Human-Machine Interaction (HMI)** within the KTU Industry 4.0 framework.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of Wearable CPS

A WCPS is typically described using a **four-layer reference architecture** aligned with the Industry 4.0 RAMI 4.0 model:

1. **Perception (Sensing) Layer**
   - Acquires raw multimodal data (bio-signals, motion, ambient).
   - Performs analogue conditioning: amplification, filtering, ADC conversion.
   - Key challenge: maintain **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) ≥ 20 dB** for clinical-grade fidelity.

2. **Network (Communication) Layer**
   - Handles short-range body-area networking (BAN) and gateway uplink.
   - Protocols: BLE 5.0 (most common), Zigbee, Wi-Fi, 5G NB-IoT.
   - Critical parameter: **data rate**, **packet loss rate**, and **energy per bit (J/bit)**.

3. **Cyber (Computation) Layer**
   - Edge processing: noise filtering (Kalman, moving average), feature extraction, ML inference.
   - Cloud processing: long-term trend analysis, model retraining.
   - Technologies: TinyML, TensorFlow Lite Micro, edge AI accelerators.

4. **Application (Actuation & Service) Layer**
   - Delivers feedback (visual, haptic, auditory) to the user.
   - Interfaces with external services (hospital EMR, fitness coach, factory MES).
   - Implements closed-loop control where required (e.g., insulin pump, exoskeleton).

### 2.2 Operational Logic Flow

- **Step 1 — Sensing:** Biosensors transduce physiological parameters into analogue voltage/current.
- **Step 2 — Pre-processing:** On-board analogue front-end amplifies weak bio-signals (e.g., ECG ≈ 1 mV peak).
- **Step 3 — Digitisation:** ADC samples the conditioned signal at frequency $f_s$.
- **Step 4 — Edge Inference:** MCU/SoC runs a trained ML model to classify the input (e.g., normal vs. arrhythmia).
- **Step 5 — Decision & Actuation:** If threshold violated, actuator is triggered (alert, drug delivery, motor control).
- **Step 6 — Communication:** Results are logged locally and transmitted to smartphone/cloud gateway.
- **Step 7 — Feedback Loop:** User/clinician may override or recalibrate, closing the loop.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Reference Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises the **high-yield quantitative relationships** a KTU examiner expects you to recognise. Memorise the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Equation / Metric | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (Nyquist) | Sampling frequency vs. signal bandwidth | ECG: $f_s \geq 500$ Hz, EMG: $f_s \geq 1000$ Hz |
| 2 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal quality index | Biomedical: $\geq 20$ dB |
| 3 | $E_{bit} = \frac{P_{tx}}{R_{b}}$ | Energy per transmitted bit | BLE: $\sim 50$ nJ/bit |
| 4 | $P_{total} = P_{sensor} + P_{MCU} + P_{comm} + P_{actuator}$ | Total power budget | Target: $< 50$ mW |
| 5 | $T_{batt} = \frac{C_{batt}\,[\text{mAh}] \cdot V_{nom}}{P_{total}\,[\text{mW}]}$ | Battery life estimation | Smartwatch: $\sim 24\text{–}72$ h |
| 6 | $\eta_{harvest} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Energy harvesting efficiency | Thermoelectric: $1\text{–}5\%$, Piezo: $5\text{–}15\%$ |
| 7 | $B_{req} = N_{ch} \cdot f_s \cdot b$ (bits/sec) | Aggregate bandwidth | Multi-modal BAN: kbps – Mbps |
| 8 | $L_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = L_{sense} + L_{proc} + L_{comm} + L_{act}$ | End-to-end latency | Closed-loop medical: $< 100$ ms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Trap Tip:** In the KTU valuation key, using `\vert` or `\mid` for *absolute value* or *conditioning* in tables is preferred over the vertical pipe `\` to avoid Markdown parser breakage. Same rule applies in your answer sheets when typesetting equations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Continuous remote patient monitoring (RPM), reducing hospital re-admissions.
- **Industrial Safety:** Fatigue detection in miners, heat-stress monitoring in steel workers.
- **Defence:** Soldier health-and-performance monitoring (DARPA programs).
- **Sports Science:** Real-time biomechanical analysis, injury prevention.
- **Assistive Tech:** Smart prosthetics, exoskeletons, rehabilitation robotics.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0 link):** Operator wearables in *Human-Cobot* collaborative cells for safe human-robot interaction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why derivations matter for KTU:** Even though this is a non-mathematical elective paper, examiners award marks for *quantitative reasoning* (Bloom's Level 3–4). Showing the derivation of power budget, battery life, or sampling requirements demonstrates higher-order understanding.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sampling Rate for a Wearable ECG Monitor

**Problem Context:** A wearable ECG patch must faithfully capture the QRS complex. The clinically meaningful bandwidth of an ECG signal is $0.5\,\text{Hz} \leq f_{max} \leq 150\,\text{Hz}$.

**Given:**
- Highest frequency component: $f_{max} = 150$ Hz.

**Find:** Minimum sampling frequency $f_s$ per Nyquist-Shannon theorem.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

By the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem, to avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest frequency present in the signal.

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

Substitute the value of $f_{max}$:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 150\,\text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_s \geq 300\,\text{Hz}}
$$

**Conclusion:** In practice, the KTU-standard clinical ECG device oversamples at $f_s = 500\,\text{Hz}$ to provide a 67% safety margin against aliasing and to allow digital filtering margin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Life Estimation for a Smart Fitness Band

**Given Specifications:**
- Battery capacity: $C_{batt} = 200\,\text{mAh}$ at $V_{nom} = 3.7\,\text{V}$.
- Sensor (PPG + IMU) power: $P_{sensor} = 8\,\text{mW}$.
- MCU active power: $P_{MCU} = 12\,\text{mW}$.
- BLE transmission power: $P_{comm} = 6\,\text{mW}$.
- Display + haptic actuator power: $P_{act} = 4\,\text{mW}$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute total average power consumption.

$$
P_{total} = P_{sensor} + P_{MCU} + P_{comm} + P_{act}
$$

$$
P_{total} = 8 + 12 + 6 + 4
$$

$$
P_{total} = 30\,\text{mW}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert battery capacity to energy in milliwatt-hours.

$$
E_{batt} = C_{batt} \cdot V_{nom}
$$

$$
E_{batt} = 200 \times 3.7
$$

$$
E_{batt} = 740\,\text{mWh}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute continuous operating time.

$$
T_{batt} = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{total}}
$$

$$
T_{batt} = \frac{740}{30}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{batt} \approx 24.67\,\text{hours}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The device can operate continuously for approximately **24.7 hours** on a full charge, which is on par with commercial fitness bands. To extend this, designers use *duty cycling* (e.g., activate sensors only 10% of the time) and *energy harvesting* from body heat or motion.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — End-to-End Latency Budget for Closed-Loop Drug Delivery

**Scenario:** An automated insulin pump (Wearable CPS for Type-1 Diabetes) must respond to a rising glucose reading before the patient's blood sugar crosses a critical threshold.

**Given:**
- CGM sensor latency: $L_{sense} = 25\,\text{ms}$.
- Edge processor inference time: $L_{proc} = 15\,\text{ms}$.
- BLE communication to pump: $L_{comm} = 20\,\text{ms}$.
- Pump mechanical actuation delay: $L_{act} = 10\,\text{ms}$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Express the total end-to-end latency.

$$
L_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = L_{sense} + L_{proc} + L_{comm} + L_{act}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
L_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = 25 + 15 + 20 + 10
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = 70\,\text{ms}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare against clinical requirement.

Clinical standard for closed-loop insulin systems (per FDA guidance) is $L_{req} \leq 100\,\text{ms}$.

$$
L_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = 70\,\text{ms} \leq L_{req} = 100\,\text{ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The design satisfies the safety margin with a 30% headroom, providing robustness against transient computational spikes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Communication Protocols for WCPS

| Protocol | Range | Data Rate | Power | Typical WCPS Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NFC** | $\leq 10$ cm | 106–424 kbps | Very low | Payment, access control |
| **BLE 5.0** | $\leq 100$ m | 2 Mbps | Low | Fitness band → phone |
| **Wi-Fi** | $\leq 50$ m | 10–100 Mbps | High | Smartwatch, AR glasses |
| **Zigbee** | $\leq 100$ m | 250 kbps | Low | Body Area Network mesh |
| **LoRa** | $\leq 10$ km | 0.3–50 kbps | Low | Remote patient monitoring (rural) |
| **5G NB-IoT** | Cellular | 200 kbps | Medium | Connected ambulance, mobile patients |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The trade-off triangle is **range vs. data rate vs. power**. BLE dominates because consumer wearables prioritise low power over long range; LoRa dominates telemedicine in remote areas.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Dec 2024]**
> **Define a Wearable Cyber-Physical System. List any four design challenges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Wearable Cyber-Physical System (WCPS) is a body-worn intelligent device that integrates **sensing, computation, communication, and actuation** into a closed-loop system for real-time monitoring and feedback of physiological or environmental parameters. **(1 mark)**

Four design challenges: **(½ mark each)**
1. **Limited power budget** — battery size is constrained by form factor.
2. **Wireless reliability** — body shadowing and motion artefacts degrade signals.
3. **Data privacy & security** — biomedical data must comply with HIPAA/GDPR.
4. **Comfort & biocompatibility** — long-term skin contact demands hypoallergenic, flexible materials.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, May 2024]**
> **Explain the role of TinyML in Wearable Cyber-Physical Systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TinyML refers to the deployment of **optimised machine learning models on ultra-low-power microcontrollers** (typically < 1 MB memory, < 1 mW inference). **(1 mark)**

In WCPS, TinyML enables:
- **On-device inference** without cloud round-trip → reduced latency and improved privacy. **(1 mark)**
- **Always-on analytics** on sensor streams (e.g., arrhythmia detection on a smartwatch) within tight energy budgets. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, *either–or* (internal choice) questions are asked. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **four-layer architecture of a Wearable Cyber-Physical System**. Explain the function of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A WCPS is organised into four cooperating layers (refer to Mermaid Diagram 4.1): **(½ mark)**

1. **Perception Layer:** Contains bio-sensors (PPG, ECG, EMG, IMU, temperature) and an analogue front-end. Function: transduce physiological/kinematic signals into digitised, conditioned data. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Network Layer:** Handles short-range wireless communication (BLE, Zigbee, Wi-Fi) to a gateway or smartphone. Function: reliable, low-energy data offload. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Cyber Computation Layer:** Runs edge AI, digital filtering, and decision logic on an MCU/SoC. Function: convert raw data into actionable insights. **(1.5 marks)**

4. **Application Layer:** Delivers feedback (haptic, visual, auditory) and interfaces with external services (cloud, clinician, industrial MES). Function: close the loop with the user/system. **(1.5 marks)**

A labelled block diagram showing **sensors → MCU → communication → actuator** with bidirectional arrows must accompany the answer. **(½ mark)**

---

**(b)** Discuss **any three real-world applications** of Wearable CPS, highlighting the physiological/physical parameter monitored and the benefit delivered. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Application | Parameter Monitored | Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM)** | Interstitial glucose via electrochemical sensor | Eliminates finger-prick tests; enables closed-loop insulin pump (artificial pancreas) for Type-1 diabetes patients. **(2.5 marks)** |
| 2 | **Industrial Fatigue-Detection Helmet** | EEG + head-motion + ambient temperature | Predicts worker drowsiness in mining/steel industries, preventing accidents and lost man-hours. **(2.5 marks)** |
| 3 | **Smart Prosthetic Limb with EMG control** | Surface EMG from residual muscles | Translates muscle intent into prosthetic motion, restoring near-natural limb function for amputees. **(2 marks)** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the **minimum sampling frequency** required for a wearable ECG patch if the highest frequency component of the QRS complex is **150 Hz**. Justify why practical systems oversample above this theoretical minimum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

By the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 150\,\text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_s \geq 300\,\text{Hz}}
$$

**(4 marks for derivation)**

Justification for oversampling (typically $f_s = 500$ Hz):
- Provides a **67% guard band** against anti-aliasing filter roll-off imperfections. **(1 mark)**
- Enables **digital decimation filtering** for higher SNR. **(1 mark)**
- Allows accurate detection of **transient artefacts** (e.g., pacemaker spikes). **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** A wearable fitness band consumes **8 mW** in sensing, **12 mW** in MCU active mode, **6 mW** in BLE transmission, and **4 mW** in display. The battery is rated **200 mAh at 3.7 V**. Calculate the **continuous operating time** and suggest **two engineering strategies** to extend it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Power:**

$$
P_{total} = 8 + 12 + 6 + 4 = 30\,\text{mW}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Battery Energy:**

$$
E_{batt} = 200 \times 3.7 = 740\,\text{mWh}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Operating Time:**

$$
T_{batt} = \frac{740}{30} \approx 24.67\,\text{hours}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Two Engineering Strategies:** **(1.5 marks each)**
1. **Duty Cycling:** Activate sensors and BLE radio only 10–20% of the time; the MCU stays in deep-sleep mode otherwise. This can reduce average power to ~6 mW, extending battery life to > 5 days.
2. **Energy Harvesting:** Integrate a thermoelectric generator (TEG) using body heat and a piezoelectric harvester using motion; together they can supplement 5–10 mW of harvested power, pushing the effective battery life beyond a week.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Wearable CPS Answers:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "IoT" with "CPS":** Many students write "Wearable IoT" instead of "Wearable CPS". KTU examiners deduct marks because IoT emphasises *connectivity*, whereas CPS emphasises the *closed-loop feedback between cyber and physical layers*. Always highlight the *feedback loop* in your diagram.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Equations like $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ are accepted, but writing $f_s = 300$ without "Hz" loses a half-mark. Always pair numbers with **SI units**.
> 
> 3. **Oversimplifying architecture:** Drawing a single box labelled "Processor" instead of distinguishing *edge processing* (on device) from *cloud analytics* costs 1–2 marks. KTU expects a **two-tier computation model** in 14-mark answers.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring privacy/security:** Applications in healthcare require HIPAA/GDPR mention. Failing to mention *data security* in any medical WCPS answer costs up to 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **No closed-loop arrow:** When asked to "draw" a CPS, students often draw one-way arrows. KTU's RAMI-4.0-aligned valuation key requires **bidirectional arrows** between the physical and cyber layers — this is a giveaway mark.
> 
> 6. **Vertical pipe misuse in tables:** Writing $\vert V \vert$ inside a markdown table breaks the parser. Use `\vert V \vert` or `\mid V \mid` to be safe.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam:**

- ✅ WCPS = **Sensors + Computation + Communication + Actuation** in a closed loop.
- ✅ The **human body** is part of the *physical* layer — distinguish this from generic IoT.
- ✅ Four-layer architecture: **Perception → Network → Cyber → Application**.
- ✅ Key design metrics: **power (< 50 mW)**, **latency (< 100 ms clinical)**, **SNR (≥ 20 dB)**, **battery life (> 24 h)**.
- ✅ Nyquist rule: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — ECG ≥ 300 Hz, EMG ≥ 1 kHz.
- ✅ Battery life formula: $T_{batt} = \dfrac{C_{batt} \cdot V_{nom}}{P_{total}}$.
- ✅ Latency budget: $L_{e2e} = L_{sense} + L_{proc} + L_{comm} + L_{act}$.
- ✅ TinyML = on-device ML for low-power inference.
- ✅ Energy per bit metric: $E_{bit} = P_{tx} / R_b$ — minimised via BLE.
- ✅ Applications: **healthcare, industry, sports, defence, assistive tech**.
- ✅ Must mention **security/privacy** in healthcare WCPS answers.
- ✅ Draw **bidirectional arrows** in architecture diagrams (closed loop).
- ✅ Always carry **SI units** in numerical answers.
- ✅ Use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of `|` inside Markdown/LaTeX tables.
